species: Southern Resident Killer Whale (S01 Pod Call)
sound_type: Repertoire-specific calls, clan dialect -- Southern Resident pod vocalizations. Discrete calls (stereotyped, pod-specific), whistles (short-range social), clicks (echolocation). Culturally transmitted acoustic repertoire. E=8.
ecosystem: Inland sea -- Puget Sound, Strait of Juan de Fuca, Salish Sea. Core range of the Southern Resident Killer Whale community.
season: Resident (year-round in core range). Summer concentration in San Juan Islands / Fraser River corridor following Chinook salmon.
original_species: Puget Sound orca S01 pod call

coordinates.description: Reference for San Juan Islands / Haro Strait -- the most critical summer foraging habitat of the Southern Resident Killer Whale community.

taxonomy_note.description: Southern Resident Killer Whale (SRKW). Orcinus orca, Family Delphinidae. Largest member of the dolphin family. The SRKW is a genetically distinct, culturally distinct population of killer whales. Three pods: J (23-25 individuals), K (18-20), L (32-35). Total approximately 73-75 individuals. ENDANGERED under US ESA (2005). Salmon-dependent (obligate Chinook eaters). Culturally transmitted acoustic dialect -- pod-specific discrete calls learned from mother and pod members. Ford 1989 established the dialect research foundation.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: First Delphinidae entry. First cetacean entry.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Orcinus: from Latin Orcus (the underworld) + -inus (belonging to). 'Belonging to the underworld' -- the ancient name for killer whales. orca: from same root. Orcinus orca = 'killer of the underworld.'
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Orcinus is monotypic -- one species in the genus. The killer whale is the sole member of Orcinus. Despite the diversity of ecotypes (resident, transient/Bigg's, offshore), all are classified as Orcinus orca. The SRKW population is one of the most studied wild cetacean populations on earth.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Cetacea, Odontoceti (toothed whales). First cetacean entry in SPS engine. The Delphinidae are the most species-diverse family in Cetacea.
taxonomy_note.family_position: First cetacean. First Delphinidae. First orca arc entry.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 500-25,000 Hz. Echolocation clicks extend well above 25,000 Hz. Most energy in discrete calls: 1,000-20,000 Hz. Whistles: typically 1,000-18,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 1,000-12,000 Hz for discrete calls. The characteristic frequency contours of SRKW discrete calls are within this range.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Discrete calls: 500-20,000 Hz, characteristic frequency contours. Whistles: 1,000-18,000 Hz, tonally pure, ascending or descending. Clicks: broadband pulses, primarily >20,000 Hz for echolocation.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual discrete calls: 1-4 seconds. Whistles: 0.5-6 seconds. Click trains: variable, used continuously during foraging. Pod vocal sessions during active foraging: continuous over many minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: DISCRETE CALLS: Stereotyped frequency contours (rising, falling, complex multi-lobed). Each call type has a characteristic shape recognizable in spectrogram. Pod-specific repertoire of 12-20+ call types. Calls are given in bouts, often overlapping between individuals.
song_metrics.note_types: Three main categories: discrete calls (social, pod-specific), whistles (social, short-range), clicks (echolocation). Plus pulsed calls (between discrete and whistle categories) and combinations.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Call bouts: multiple individuals calling simultaneously or in sequence. Superpod calls: all three pods' repertoires overlapping. High-energy foraging sessions: rapid, overlapping calls at high rate.
song_metrics.timbre: Discrete calls: squeaky, electronic, alien-sounding to human ears -- the characteristic 'orca sound.' Whistles: smooth, tonal, flute-like. Clicks: rapid broadband pulses, not melodic.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=8 -- MAXIMUM. Pod vocalizations are acoustically overwhelming in proximity. Carry for kilometers underwater. Detectable by hydrophone arrays from very long distances. The sound of an orca pod is one of the most powerful and complex natural acoustic events in the marine environment.
song_metrics.tempo: Variable. Rapid during active foraging. Slower during resting and traveling. Superpod events: high density, high rate.
song_metrics.context: SOCIAL COORDINATION AND IDENTITY. Discrete calls primarily social: maintain group cohesion, coordinate foraging, reinforce social bonds, identify pod membership. Southern Residents are more vocal when foraging than traveling or resting -- contrast with transient orca at degree 272 (tactical silence during hunting).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Pod-specific repertoires: J, K, L pods each have distinct call types not shared with other pods. Some calls shared across the community; others pod-specific. The acoustic dialect maps onto social structure.
song_metrics.key_property: THREE-QUARTERS MARK (270/360 = 75%). ORCA ARC BEGINS. First cetacean, first Delphinidae in SPS engine. Southern Resident Killer Whale: ENDANGERED, ~73-75 individuals, J/K/L pods, salmon-dependent (obligate Chinook), culturally transmitted dialect. E=8. E=8 on BOTH SIDES at 270 (The Fartz, S36). Maximum energy at the engine's three-quarters milestone.

plumage.overall: Striking black and white countershading. Dorsal surface jet black; ventral surface brilliant white. Distinctive white oval patch behind and above each eye. Grey saddle patch behind dorsal fin (saddle patch shape varies by individual -- used for photo-identification).
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Adult males: large dorsal fin, up to 1.8m tall, distinctive erect profile. Females/juveniles: smaller, more curved dorsal fin. Southern Resident saddle patches are 'open' (lighter grey, partially open to the dorsal surface) vs. Bigg's orca saddle patches which tend to be 'closed.' Saddle patch differences allow visual identification of ecotype at sea.
plumage.seasonal_variation: No seasonal plumage variation. Year-round coloration consistent.
plumage.flight_pattern: Not applicable. Surface behaviors: breaching (leaping fully clear of water), spy-hopping (rising vertically to look above surface), tail-lobbing, pec-slapping. Surface behaviors are visible acoustic events.

physical_description.overall: Adult males: 6-8m length, 3,600-5,400 kg. Adult females: 5-7m length, 1,400-3,600 kg. Largest member of Family Delphinidae. Highly sexually dimorphic: male dorsal fins reach 1.8m; female dorsal fins 0.9m. Distinctive black and white coloration with individual-specific saddle patches.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Large black and white cetacean with erect dorsal fin (males), large saddle patch, white eye patch. In the Salish Sea: only species with this combination. Unmistakable.
physical_description.field_id: Adult male dorsal fin is diagnostic -- tall, erect, up to 1.8m. Black dorsal surface, white eye patch, white ventral surface. Blows visible from distance. Southern Residents often in tight family groups, calling frequently.

nodes.title: Three-Quarters Mark + Orca Arc Begins
nodes.description: 270/360 = 75%. E=8 on both sides. The most significant structural milestone since the halfway point. The orca enters the engine at maximum energy at the three-quarters mark.

nodes.title: Cultural Dialect -- Learned, Not Encoded
nodes.description: SRKW pod-specific calls are culturally transmitted (learned from mother/pod). Not genetically encoded. Animal culture in the strict biological sense. John Ford 1989.

nodes.title: Endangered + Salmon Dependence
nodes.description: ~73-75 individuals. ESA 2005. Obligate Chinook salmon eaters. Chinook decline = SRKW nutritional stress. Cross-reference to salmon arc degrees 236-242.

nodes.title: Orca Arc -- Seven Degrees
nodes.description: Degrees 270-276. Pod vocalization (270), whistles/clicks (271), transient hunt (272), arc continues. The emotional centerpiece of the SPS engine.

cross_references.relationship: ORCA ARC CONTINUES. Degree 270 documents pod call repertoire; degree 271 focuses on whistles and complex echolocation clicks. Two consecutive SRKW entries.
ECOTYPE CONTRAST. Southern Resident (salmon-dependent, vocal) vs. Bigg's/transient (mammal-eating, acoustically silent during hunting). Two ecotypes, opposite acoustic strategies.
HABITAT TRANSITION. Forest edge (bird community, E=7) to inland sea (cetacean community, E=8). Both community acoustic events.
